channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Can Innovative Nicotine Products Improve Family Life?
item.description: Innovative nicotine products can affect more than individual smokers — they can also benefit families and households. When adults switch away from combustible cigarettes, exposure to smoke, odour and smoking-related harm may be reduced for those around them. Harm reduction is not about ignoring risk, but about choosing safer pathways where nicotine use already exists. Public health policy should recognise the wider benefits of smoke-free alternatives for consumers, families and communities. (Published on June 18, 2026bybrusselsmorning.com)

item.title: An interview with Derek Yach about his recent report: "Nicotine 2030"
item.description: Global tobacco policy is still struggling to separate nicotine use from the harms of smoking. A harm reduction approach recognises that adults who cannot or do not want to quit nicotine should still have access to safer alternatives than combustible cigarettes. The challenge is not only scientific, but institutional: evidence on relative risk often clashes with entrenched ideology. Public health should focus on reducing disease and death, not defending outdated assumptions about nicotine. (Published on June 18, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Challenging the Obsession About Youth Uptake, Data Show That US Tobacco Policy Is Focusing on the Wrong Issue
item.description: US tobacco policy remains heavily focused on youth nicotine uptake, even as smoking continues to cause the greatest harm. Recent data suggest that youth vaping has fallen substantially, while adult smokers still need realistic routes away from combustible cigarettes. Protecting young people is essential, but it should not come at the cost of blocking harm reduction for adults. Public health policy should follow the scale of harm, not only the loudest political concern. (Published on June 18, 2026byvapingpost.com)

item.title: South Africa’s Youth Vaping Crisis: What the Study and Headlines Leave Out
item.description: Before accepting dominant narratives on nicotine, the evidence deserves closer examination. Claims about vaping, youth uptake or harm reduction often become policy arguments before the data are properly tested. When evidence shows declining smoking, falling youth vaping or major differences in relative risk, public health should adjust its assumptions. Better nicotine policy starts with asking whether the narrative fits the facts. (Published on June 18, 2026bythrglobal.org)
